description:
DBS is a leading financial services group in Asia with a presence in 19 markets. Headquartered and listed in Singapore, DBS is in the three key Asian axes of growth: Greater China, Southeast Asia and South Asia. The bank's "AA-" and "Aa1" credit ratings are among the highest in the world.

Recognised for its global leadership, DBS has been named “World’s Best Bank” by Global Finance, “World’s Best Bank” by Euromoney and “Global Bank of the Year” by The Banker. The bank is at the forefront of leveraging digital technology to shape the future of banking, having been named “World’s Best Digital Bank” by Euromoney and the world’s “Most Innovative in Digital Banking” by The Banker. In addition, DBS has been accorded the “Safest Bank in Asia“ award by Global Finance for 14 consecutive years from 2009 to 2022.

DBS provides a full range of services in consumer, SME and corporate banking. As a bank born and bred in Asia, DBS understands the intricacies of doing business in the region’s most dynamic markets. DBS is committed to building lasting relationships with customers, as it banks the Asian way. Through the DBS Foundation, the bank creates impact beyond banking by supporting social enterprises: businesses with a double bottom-line of profit and social and/or environmental impact. DBS Foundation also gives back to society in various ways, including equipping communities with future-ready skills and building food resilience.

With its extensive network of operations in Asia and emphasis on engaging and empowering its staff, DBS presents exciting career opportunities. For more information, please visit www.dbs.com

“Hello? Please help me. I think… I was scammed.” 

I’ve been a customer service officer for over seven years and handled tens of thousands of calls, but the hardest ones are always the fraud cases. 

There’s a pause at the start sometimes. Or a breath you can hear catching. And then the story comes out – fragmented, rushed, sometimes hard to follow. 

In those moments, my first job is to help them feel steady again. 

I slow things down. I let them speak. I listen for what they’re trying to say, even when the details don’t come in order. And I remind them they’re not alone – we’re here, and we’ll figure this out together. 

At the same time, I’m piecing everything together. Capturing the details, asking the right questions, making sure nothing gets missed.  

That used to mean splitting my attention in every direction at once. 

In 2023, I was part of the pilot for Customer Service Officer (CSO) Assistant. I wanted to see if AI could actually help in moments like these. 

It wasn’t perfect at the start. We tested it, challenged it, and kept feeding back to make it better. 

Today, it’s the co-pilot to over 1,000 of us. It takes care of the transcribing and surfaces what I need, so I don’t have to keep shifting my focus. 

I can stay more present with the customer to make sure they feel heard, not just answered.  

On fraud calls, that makes all the difference. 

Of course, most calls aren’t as emotional. Many are everyday banking requests. But the expectation is always the same. When customers call, they need clarity, and they need it quickly. 

Now, when a call comes in, I can see what’s going on almost immediately, what happened before, what they might need next.  

You’d think after a full day on the phone, I wouldn’t want to talk anymore. Maybe that was true once. 

But these days, I notice something different. I’m better now at listening for what’s underneath the words.  

With my two kids, aged 10 and five, I can tell how their day was from the way they say, “Hello, Mummy.” Sometimes it’s quick and bright. Sometimes it’s softer. 

Over dinner or on the couch, I ask about their day and listen the same way I do with my customers: patiently, carefully, and with the reassurance that I’m always here. 

— Fereal Feroz Abdullah, Customer Service Officer #HumansofDBS

Do you really need to sound smart at work? DBS CEO Tan Su Shan explains why trying to be clever can get in the way of learning - and why asking better questions often matters more than having all the answers.

"We have a full spectrum in our entire wealth management continuum...to serve our clients wherever they start and wherever they continue to progress. This is the wealth management continuum that we've built in DBS." – Tse Koon Shee, Group Executive and Head, Consumer Banking and Wealth Management Group

Years ago, ambitious professionals, executives and entrepreneurs sat in an in-between space. Too complex for traditional mass affluent banking, yet not always aligned to private banking models built around larger AUM thresholds.

In response, DBS created an industry-first dedicated segment, Treasures Private Client, to serve this gap.

DBS Treasures Private Client has since evolved alongside clients as their ambitions, portfolios and needs have grown.

Last month, we celebrated 15 years of Treasures Private Client and unveiled a refreshed proposition anchored on four pillars:

1) Portfolio Advisory: A holistic view of wealth to identify risks, concentrations and opportunities.

2) Access: Broader solutions, from private markets to emerging asset classes traditionally reserved for larger pools of capital.

3) Intelligence: In addition to our Chief Investment Office which is among the most respected in Asia, enhanced AI-powered insights enabling timely and relevant advice.

4) Experience & Community: Connecting clients to a trusted network of accomplished individuals and business leaders to exchange ideas.

As Asia’s wealth landscape evolves, so does the way we support those building it. We are grateful for the trust our clients place in us and remain committed to being a partner at every stage of the wealth journey.

#DBSTreasuresPrivateClient

When uncertainty becomes the business reality, trust matters more than ever.

After seven years as a Relationship Manager (RM), Danial now leads a team of RMs supporting SME clients across F&B, construction and pharmaceuticals – businesses that operate at the heart of the real economy. 

As market conditions tightened and operating costs rose, many clients shifted priorities from pursuing growth to ensuring business continuity and resilience. For businesses reliant on transportation and raw materials, ongoing supply chain disruptions meant difficult decisions, including delaying new commitments.

In moments like these, clients don’t just need financing – they need a bank that understands their business, anticipates risks and stands with them through change. For Danial and his team, DBS’s suite of AI tools enables them to gain deeper, faster insights into each client’s operations so that they are able to lead meaningful conversations and offer advice grounded in context.

“AI helps us surface opportunities and risks that may not be immediately obvious. It also frees me up to engage clients meaningfully, guide my team and create space for life beyond work,” he shares.

By embracing change as a constant, Danial and his team turn uncertainty into an opportunity to learn, grow and move forward with confidence.

Explore how you can build a fulfilling career at DBS: https://go.dbs.com/4w0KVBn

#LifeAtDBS #LiveFulfilled

What does leadership look like in a world defined by uncertainty and rapid technological change? 

At CNBC Converge recently, DBS CEO Tan Su Shan shared two clear priorities: building trust and using technology responsibly.

Cybersecurity is now a defining risk – a new battleground that requires constant vigilance. 

AI is unlocking immense opportunity, but success will depend on safe, smart adoption and strong human judgement

Organisations have a responsibility to equip people with the skills to adapt, because ultimately, people must choose to lean in and grow.

As industries navigate macro volatility and digital transformation, one thing is clear: how we adopt technology will matter as much as how fast we do it.
 
At DBS, this continues to shape our approach as an AI-enabled bank with a heart — building resilience, strengthening trust and enabling our people to thrive. 
 
Click through the images for more on what Su Shan said.

Photos: CNBC

"Managing change and transforming with AI have been key parts of our engine and DNA for well over a decade to derive data-led insights, improve customer journeys, remove employee toil and strengthen business resiliency.” – Derrick Goh, Group Chief Operating Officer, DBS 

Following a recent visit to Silicon Valley, Derrick shared his reflections on AI’s ability to unlock a whole new world full of possibilities in a guest article for Euromoney.

He highlighted how GenAI is enabling DBS to fundamentally redesign our operating model through human–AI collaboration. Built over the past decade, our data and AI foundation has allowed us to systematically re‑engineer processes and workflows, upskill and reskill our people, and reorganise teams to work alongside AI agents.

Today, over 70% of DBS employees actively use our in‑house DBS‑GPT, with many others creating personal and team agents. From AI assistants and chatbots for customer service, to coding agents for development, testing and deployment, as well as documentary AI – GenAI is now deeply embedded in how we work.

Said Derrick, "It can feel scary when the ground and everything seem to be shifting every day by AI innovations. However, AI can be a resounding win for employees, customers and the organisation – we just need to get our basics right and steer the change carefully.” 

Read his article here: https://lnkd.in/gfJMFq56

Singapore's ranking in DBS' Global AI Financial Hub Index is encouraging, but should also be seen as a call to action, said Deputy Prime Minister Gan Kim Yong.
 
Speaking at the DBS Leaders dialogue last week, he said, "This morning’s launch of the Global AI Financial Hub Index is timely. Singapore’s ranking ... is a strong vote of confidence. But more importantly, it tells us where the next race will be fought."
 
DPM Gan stressed three things Singapore must do well for our next bound as a financial centre. They were: translating AI from experimentation into enterprise-wide use, creating good jobs and opportunities, and ensuring trust, safety and security are built into how AI is developed and applied. 
 
"This is how Singapore can compete – not by chasing every AI trend, but by becoming a trusted base for high-impact AI adoption in finance," he said.

"As you incorporate AI into your operations, bring people – your customers and colleagues – with you because culture starts from the top."– DBS CEO Tan Su Shan 
 
Speaking at a DBS Leaders dialogue on AI and Trust, Su Shan reflected on DBS' agentic AI journey and shared three key learnings, including the need to "bring your people with you". 

She also spoke about putting data in order and the importance of learning by doing. 

"As a regulated entity, we have a responsibility to keep our customers’ money and data safe. But we create controlled spaces for people to experiment and try, such as through hackathons," she said, adding "it was in this room where 300 of my leadership team were building their own agents because humans learn best by doing".

company_updates.description:
“I was in my 20s, living in Hong Kong and working at one of the Big 4s. New city, new culture… even a new relationship (Spoiler: she’s now my wife!). 

Life was full.

Then, someone asked me a question that changed everything. “Would you ever consider returning to Singapore to work for DBS?”

That someone was Peng Khim, then Group Head of Technology for IBG. (He’s now Chief Technology Officer for Applications.) I first got to know him as a client, then as a mentor. So when he asked me that question, I really sat up and listened. 

At that time, I was working as a tech consultant in Hong Kong – combining my background in corporate finance and technology to oversee digital transformation projects for banks and corporates. 

One of my clients was DBS Hong Kong. The bank was migrating its core banking system from a mainframe to an open system. 

Peng Khim flew in from Singapore to provide leadership advisory on the project. I remember being in awe of his work ethic and expertise, and how invested he was in getting things right. He would explain the products to me in great detail, down to the flows and processes. 

We clicked quite naturally. Maybe it was because we were both Singaporeans working away from home. Even after the project ended, we stayed in touch.

So when he reached out about a role at DBS, I took it seriously.  

It wasn’t an easy decision. Saying yes meant leaving Hong Kong – and asking my partner to make that move with me. 

Still, I was drawn to DBS and the major progress it was making on the digital front. 

My partner and I spent weeks talking it through. In the end, I took the leap. 

I joined as an Engineering Sales Specialist in IBGT, working closely with the business to pitch and deliver technology solutions to clients.

Over time, my scope of work expanded, leading GTS platforms across key domains like Cash, Trade, Securities & Fiduciary Services, Sustainability and Customer Management and Analytics. 

As the tech landscape evolved, especially with Gen AI, I had to learn quickly – reading, experimenting and honestly, just learning on the job. It felt unfamiliar at times, but also exciting in the same way Hong Kong once did.

Eight years in, I now lead a team of over 100 people to build AI-driven solutions across the business. As a technologist, I still enjoy being hands-on, using AI tools to help build applications. 

Between work and raising a two-year-old, the days go by quickly.

Life is still full – just in a different way.”

- Devin Ong, Executive Director, Institutional Banking Group Technology, DBS #HumansofDBS

Today that vision has evolved into something much broader – a full wealth ecosystem bringing together the strength of DBS’ private banking, institutional banking and digital capabilities under one proposition.

What if your first job isn’t about catching up but accelerating ahead? 
 
For most graduates, the early years of a career are spent learning the ropes. But with AI, that’s changing fast. 
 
At the 2026 Institute of Banking and Finance Financial Industry Fiesta, Clarissa represented DBS in a fireside chat with Deputy Prime Minister Gan Kim Yong – sharing how her career journey didn’t follow a typical path but one shaped by continuous growth.
 
Across three internships with the bank through her university work-study programme, she took on increasingly complex work with each return. Strong mentorship and a culture that encourages learning by doing ultimately drew her back as a full-time Data Scientist through the Management Associate (MA) programme. 
 
Clarissa shares, “What stood out for me at DBS is the trust placed in young employees like me to learn and take on meaningful work early on.”
 
But it was the rise of AI that truly accelerated her growth. 
 
“AI tools have really changed the pace at which I can learn and contribute. Instead of spending time on routine tasks, I can now focus more on work that traditionally would have taken me much longer to grow into,” she adds.
 
With in-house AI tools like CodeBuddy and DBS-GPT embedded into her workflow, her role quickly evolved beyond technical execution, expanding her capacity to engage in stakeholder management and decision-making earlier in her career.
 
Across DBS, close to 1,600 young talents have joined through internship, traineeship and MA programmes between 2024 and 2026. As AI continues to evolve, so will the way careers are built – faster, more dynamic and with greater impact from the start. 
 
Clarissa’s experience reflects our commitment to investing early in the next generation of talent, building a pipeline of future-ready graduates in this AI-enabled world.
 
Discover a fulfilling career with DBS today: https://go.dbs.com/4wGDsrJ
 
#LifeAtDBS #LiveFulfilled
 
[Photo credit: Institute of Banking and Finance Singapore]

Singapore’s traditional value proposition has been, “We are trusted because we don’t make mistakes.” But in a world of accelerating change, DBS CEO Tan Su Shan offers a necessary shift. “We must evolve to become trusted because we are the safest place to make mistakes.”
 
"Can we be a testbed for new products or help corporates and clients expand into new markets and develop new products to move up the value and supply chain?" she asked. 
 
Su Shan shared this at a DBS leaders dialogue today, with Singapore Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Trade and Industry Gan Kim Yong as Guest of Honour. In her welcome address, she spoke about how trust must evolve alongside AI – from preserving stability to enabling safe experimentation at scale. She also reflected on DBS' journey in a more agentic AI world, underscoring the importance of bringing people along and recognising that we learn best by doing.
 
This theme of trust in the age of AI carried into the fireside chat with DPM Gan. He spoke about what continues to anchor Singapore’s edge: trust built over decades, the ability to move with speed and agility, and a sustained commitment to investing in people as AI reshapes jobs. He also addressed growing concerns around security and resilience as AI adoption accelerates.
 
At the dialogue, DBS also launched The Trusted AI Financial Hub – a report on how AI, governance and trust are reshaping competition among global financial centres. It also introduced a new index comparing 15 major financial hubs across these factors. Read the report here: https://go.dbs.com/4980KwC 
 
"The launch of the Global AI Financial Hub Index is timely. Singapore's ranking – third among major financial hubs, behind New York and San Francisco – is a strong vote of confidence," said DPM Gan. "While we are encouraged by Singapore’s ranking, we should also see it as a call to action." 
 
At DBS, we believe progress in AI must go hand in hand with responsibility. Building systems that are trusted, transparent, and designed with people at the centre. As DPM Gan put it, “The issue is not whether AI is good or bad. AI is powerful – and powerful technologies require effective safeguards.”

“We want to reconcile some of the dilemmas facing the human being with the promise of the technology – coming up with a narrative that brings both viewpoints together.” – Chee Kin Lam, Group Head of Legal and Compliance, DBS
 
Speaking on a recent podcast with TRM Labs, Chee Kin shared how DBS approaches AI – not as a tension between people and technology but as a way to bring the two together.

At DBS, every AI use case starts with a simple question: Why should the employee do this? From using LLMs to sift through large datasets to drafting repetitive reports, Chee Kin highlighted how AI is applied deliberately, with a clear employee value proposition.

“No, cannot. You must get a degree first.” 

That was what my parents said when I told them I wanted to be a pilot.

They weren’t against the dream – they just didn’t want me to start working without a degree to fall back on.

So I made them a promise: I’d get my degree first, then apply to the airlines.

For as long as I can remember, I had built my life around flying. I graduated from polytechnic with a near‑perfect GPA in Aviation and Business, convinced I was finally ready to head for the cockpit.

But first, National Service.

I jumped at the chance to apply for the Air Force, hoping it might lead to a career in aviation. Instead, I failed the medical assessment due to a minor condition – the criteria were much stricter for the military, as compared to commercial flying.

I completed my two years of service and went on to pursue a degree in Business and Marketing at the National University of Singapore. Determined to keep my promise to dad, I took classes every semester with no breaks, finishing my honours degree in three years instead of four.

I thought I was speeding towards my dream – but the timing could not have been worse.

By the time I graduated in June 2020, the world had changed. Covid-19 hit, the aviation industry froze, and that path was no longer realistic. Peers who had joined airlines ahead of me were laid off one by one.

Clearly, I was not going to be a pilot. I also couldn’t find a job related to what I studied in university.

So, I started over.

Software engineering felt like a good next step. I had no coding background, and honestly, it felt like starting from zero. I spent weeks learning on my own, studying university textbook that I had borrowed from friends.

I applied what I learnt to build my first portfolio website, and it helped me secure an SGUnited traineeship with DBS later that same year. 

From there, I joined the DBS Skill Enhancement Education & Development (SEED) programme, which led me to a full‑time job at DBS as a software engineer in 2021.

The learning curve was steep. There were times I wondered if I – a self-trained engineer – really belonged. Over time, I realised that no one really has everything figured out – you just keep learning, keep trying, and slowly get better.

Today, I work on cross-border payments systems, building backend services that support real transactions across countries. It’s a space where things have to work – not just in theory, but in real life.

As the systems grew, I started using AI tools to help with testing, debugging, and validation – the repetitive parts of the work – so I could spend more time thinking through design and solving more complex problems.

I think of AI as a useful co-pilot – it can reduce toil and execute my commands, but it doesn’t replace judgement. At the end of the day, it’s not just about the code. I ‘m still responsible for where things go. I’m still the one flying the plane.

(Continued in the comments)

Yesterday, we announced an SGD 10 million support package to help Singaporeans and local SMEs/SMBs navigate today’s cost pressures. This complements the Singapore Government’s SGD 1 billion support package, as we collectively work to help the community manage rising living costs.
 
From August to December 2026, the SGD 10 million package will deliver more than three million cashback redemptions to help Singaporeans defray everyday expenses like groceries and meals.  Among these, is the return of our popular SGD 3 cashback every Saturday which supports hawker stalls and heartland shops.
 
We are also enhancing support for SMEs/SMBs through improved guidance and broader access to over 16,000 AI solution providers, via initiatives like the enhanced Spark GenAI programme. This will reduce AI adoption friction and accelerate its implementation for real business value.
 
Announcing the support package, Mr Jeffrey Siow, Acting Minister for Transport and Senior Minister of State for Finance, said, “In Singapore, we all pull together to help one another – Government, businesses, and society. This is how we deal with troubles and crisis together. This way we always make sure that no Singaporean is left to bear the burden alone."
 
DBS Singapore Country Head Him Chuan LIM 林鑫川 added, “As a purpose-driven bank, DBS/POSB is guided by our belief that we are not just present in good times but also step forward when it matters. We hope this gesture of encouragement will go some way in providing practical support to those who need it as we tide over these times together.”
 
Mr Siow, who is also Member of Parliament for Chua Chu Kang GRC, and Him Chuan were joined at the launch by Dr Choo Pei Ling, Member of Parliament for Chua Chu Kang GRC, alongside other senior leaders from DBS and POSB.

In an AI‑enabled era, a manager’s role isn’t to have all the answers. It’s to empower teams to learn, experiment and move forward with confidence.

For Noelle, a Team Lead in Project Management within Group Technology at DBS, AI plays an important role in strengthening performance and supporting professional growth. As a project and people manager, she shares that “the most meaningful part of my role is enabling my team to succeed – both in delivering outcomes and in growing their capabilities and confidence.”

Working with complex systems and tight timelines, Noelle encourages her team to embed AI into everyday workflows to create clarity, reduce manual effort and mitigate risks. She also uses AI insights as the starting point for team discussions, reviewing the outputs and validating assumptions together. “What would it take for you to resign from DBS and join us?”
 
I had prepared for many questions ahead of this client meeting - but definitely not this one.
 
It came at the end of a long afternoon, where my team and I had shared how DBS uses data and AI to enhance customer service at scale. Someone asked it casually, almost as an aside.
 
I didn’t answer straight away. It got me thinking.
 
DBS has been my first and only job since I joined 26 years ago, fresh out of polytechnic at the age of 23. I’ve never really thought about leaving. I don’t even have a LinkedIn profile.
 
I started out at the Liat Towers branch as a Relationship Customer Service Officer, taking calls day in, day out. With each call, I learnt what mattered most to customers - because they shared it with you, every day. 
 
Over the years, I moved through different roles, from Trainer to Team Manager, then credit operations. From 2014 to 2017, I was travelling frequently between India and Indonesia to help set up digital banking in new markets. There were no legacy systems, no physical branches to fall back on. We were building from scratch. 
 
That’s where the idea of “designing for no calls” really took shape.
 
If we understood why customers reached out, why not address those needs beforehand?
 
It led us to build self-service journeys for everyday requests - blocking a lost card, replacing it, adjusting transfer limits - with actions customers could take immediately and conveniently. 
 
That way of thinking stayed with me when I returned to DBS Singapore. 
 
Today, as the Gen AI team lead in the Customer Service Hub, it sits at the centre of my work. I work closely with teams who review conversations and check AI-generated responses. 
 
Technology can surface patterns and draft answers. But someone still has to decide whether a response makes sense, whether it sounds right and whether it will truly help the person on the other end. 
 
That judgement doesn’t come from the system. It comes from understanding how customers think and how frontline work really happens. 
 
So when that question came up at the end of the meeting, it made me pause. 
 
Not because I was thinking of leaving. I wasn’t. DBS is where I’ve chosen to build my career, across different roles and phases. 
 
Yet, I’d never thought of the work I’d been doing quietly for years, across call centres, digital banks and now Gen AI, could become so relevant and sought after. 
 
The next morning was mostly routine. But as I logged in, I felt a quiet sense of renewed energy, and pride in the work I do. 

- Ali Jinah Mohamed Yusuf, Gen AI Team Lead (Customer Service Hub)

“It often starts with a test – a small transfer of a few dollars, just to check whether someone will comply and follow instructions. 

After that, follow-up attempts often appear, each one in quicker succession, in larger amounts, and sometimes involving different bank accounts.  

In many government impersonation scams, victims are prompted to make small ‘trial’ transfers, just to see if trust has been built.   
After that, scammers escalate quickly, guiding victims toward much larger transfer attempts.  

These are some of the patterns I look out for in my work as DBS. I work on the bank’s 24/7 fraud monitoring systems, which use a combination of AI/ML models and rules-based controls. 

Our enhanced surveillance capabilities enabled us to intervene more effectively to prevent significant losses in cases that featured unique modus operandi. 

This is just one example of how my work sits at the intersection of data, technology and human behaviour. 

The AI/ML models analyse hundreds of data points to assess risk, while the rules help surface specific behaviours that need closer attention. 

With the proliferation of AI, people often assume that computers do all the work. But that’s not true.  

A machine can tell you how many transactions happened or assign a risk score. But what it doesn’t understand is why someone keeps trying after a failed transfer – the urgency, fear and authority scammers deliberately create. 

A big part of my work is understanding the psychology behind scams. That insight shapes how we design our monitoring – not just to detect suspicious activity, but to intervene at the moment when customers are most vulnerable.  

Sometimes that can mean tightening detection, or it can be as simple as slowing things down and introducing purposeful friction, like a temporary freeze on the account if too many successive transfers are attempted, so customers have time to pause and think before acting. 

What makes the work meaningful isn’t just building smarter systems. It’s knowing that behind every tweak and adjustment, there’s a real person who might be spared a devastating loss, often without ever knowing how close they came to it. 
 
That’s what an AI enabled bank with a heart looks like to me. 

AI helps us see more and act faster. But it’s people who understand context, behaviour and consequence that give those signals meaning.” 

- Seah Pei Rong, Fraud Monitoring and Surveillance Team

“I thought I would stay for six months, or at most, maybe a year. Just long enough to get some working experience during my gap year before university.

Well, it has been over six years – and I’m still with DBS.

I started out in the customer centre, as a Customer Service Officer (CSO), taking calls and helping people with their day‑to‑day banking needs. 

Then, I moved into mentoring, guiding new joiners as they found their footing. And just when I started to feel comfortable, something new that piqued my interest would come along.

New opportunities. New challenges. New things to try.

So year after year, I stayed at the bank.

But that didn’t mean putting my studies on hold. Instead, I found a way to do both. 

I enrolled in a part-time bachelor’s degree in Business Studies, which I’ll be completing later this year. 

Today, my role has evolved into a Gen AI Evaluator. When I told my family, they were surprised. To them, AI belonged in young tech startups – not in a bank that’s been around since the 1960s.

My job is to test and evaluate AI tools used in the customer centre – from those already supporting our teams today to new ones still taking shape. 

One of my earliest projects was providing feedback on the CSO Assistant, a Gen AI virtual assistant tool that today supports our 1,000 CSOs with over 250,000 queries monthly.  

Because I had worked as a CSO myself, I drew on that experience to consider whether the AI-generated responses would truly support how CSOs work. 

Beyond pilots and initial testing, I review and evaluate AI-powered tools that are already live – including our digibot chatbot, going through customer interactions every day to ensure responses remain accurate and relevant. 

Then there’s the more experimental side of the work. 

As the bank continues to innovate new tools, I’m currently helping to train a voice bot for customers – spending hours running simulated customer interactions, putting myself in our customers’ shoes and considering the many different ways a question might be phrased. 

In one of my recent modules in school, we looked at real-world case studies on how organisations are scaling and embedding AI into everyday work. Much of it felt familiar, because I was already living this at DBS.

During lectures, the focus is on the big picture – the high-level strategy and intent behind embracing AI. At work, I get to see what actually brings these concepts to life: the rigorous testing, the multiple checks, and all the other moving parts that are invisible to most.

School gave me the language for things I had already been practising at work. And work gave meaning to what I was learning in class.” 

- Shafira Nurmuzaliffah bte Mohd Mydin-Watt, Gen AI Evaluator (Customer Centre)

In one of my recent modules in school, we looked at real-world case studies on how organisations are scaling and embedding AI into everyday work. Much of it felt familiar, because I was already living this at DBS.

During lectures, the focus is on the big picture – the high-level strategy and intent behind embracing AI. At work, I get to see what actually brings these concepts to life: the rigorous testing, the multiple checks, and all the other moving parts that are invisible to most.

School gave me the language for things I had already been practising at work. And work gave meaning to what I was learning in class.” 

- Shafira Nurmuzaliffah bte Mohd Mydin-Watt, Gen AI Evaluator (Customer Centre)

“I like to joke that I prefer talking to my computer rather than to people – and that’s not entirely untrue. 

I’ve always been an introvert – I’m most comfortable looking at data, breaking problems down, and figuring out how things work. That’s just how I’m wired (no pun intended!), and it’s one reason why I’ve stayed in the Group Procurement team at DBS over the past 15 years.

Even though I’ve stayed within the same team, my role has evolved over time, from data and reporting to financial coordination and strategic planning and now encompasses driving AI/Gen AI initiatives.

Each shift gave me a broader view of how procurement works end to end, and more chances to apply what I do best: looking at data, breaking problems down, and understanding how different parts of the process fit together.

So when AI began to take centre stage over the past few years, talking to (yet another) computer felt natural to me.  

I remember taking an AI module way back in university, when it was mostly theory. AI then was far removed from how people actually worked. So, after I graduated, my career moved into data and business roles within the bank. 

In 2022, when AI evolved exponentially, so did my role at DBS. I focused on AI enablement, leading and monitoring the models we call an “AI control tower” within Procurement.  

Just like how the control tower at the airport monitors all flights from a single vantage point, helping ensure everything runs safely and smoothly, I look across the department’s workflows to identify where AI can be applied meaningfully and safely. 

That includes automating manual checks, proactively monitoring supplier risks, and helping teams surface information more efficiently so they can make better decisions.  

A big part of it is understanding the processes end to end and deciding if AI is even the right tool in the first place. When it is, I work closely with technology and data chapter partners to design use cases that are governed properly, tested carefully, and deployed responsibly, always with humans still in the loop. 

Over the years, I’ve seen how much effort goes into repetitive checks and manual work. It’s time and effort that could be better spent elsewhere.

If a system can reliably handle repetitive, structured tasks, it allows people to focus on judgment, decision-making and higher value work – the things humans are actually best at. 

Looking ahead, I’m especially curious about agentic AI – systems that don’t just respond, but can plan and act with intent. We’re not fully there yet, and there are important considerations around governance, controls and oversight.  

Still, I’m very excited to be a part of an organisation that is proactively and thoughtfully reimagining the future of fintech – where my computer talks back to me, for real.” 

– Lin Liping, AI, Control Tower and Planning Team Lead (Group Procurement & Services) #IWD2026 #HumansofDBS

If I were working a regular nine-to-five job, I’d definitely see my family and my girlfriend more often. 

I work in DBS’ Operations Monitoring Centre (in the room you see behind me), in the team that provides 24-hour support to the bank’s 1,200-strong ATM network.  

You can think of us like air traffic control – except instead of planes, we’re watching the “traffic” of 1,200 ATMs across the island, like how frequently they’re used by our customers.

To do this, we rely on multiple dashboards powered by AI/ML. These tools help detect patterns and early signals – where cash levels are dropping faster than usual, which machines are nearing capacity with deposits or coins, or which ones will soon need servicing – so we don’t have to manually track every machine. 

Instead, we can focus our time on interpreting what we’re seeing and deciding what action to take, like coordinating with cash‑in‑transit auxiliary police officers and engineers often before any issue becomes visible on the ground.

If we do our job right, most customers will never see the complexity behind the scenes. They’ll just simply walk up to an ATM, withdraw or deposit their cash, and move on with their day – and that’s exactly the experience we’re aiming for.

Working in this role has also meant learning new skills. Understanding how to read data, trusting machine‑generated signals and working alongside AI‑driven tools is now part of the job. 

Of course, as you’d expect, there are daily peak periods – lunchtime, morning and evening rush hours.
 
There are also peak seasons like the two weeks before Chinese New Year, where we add an additional 71 pop-up ATM machines to the network so customers can withdraw their notes for gifting, and Li Chun, where people deposit money based on their zodiac’s lucky hours. 
 
To support 24/7 coverage of the ATM network, the team runs on fixed 12-hour shifts – either 6am to 6pm or 6pm to 6am. We usually work three days on and two days off, rotating between day and night shifts.  
 
I’m now three years into this role at DBS, but this was my first foray into shift work, so it took quite a bit of time to get used to.  
 
My first few shifts were especially hard. I had to pick up practical habits along the way from my colleagues – things like using blackout curtains to sleep at 7am and taking short naps before night shifts.
 
We get our schedule months in advance, so nothing’s a surprise, but since I wasn’t much of a planner to begin with, I found I wasn’t seeing my family or my girlfriend as much as I used to. (They work regular office hours).

So I’ve had to be far more intentional with my time. 

The same habits I learnt on the job – planning ahead, paying attention to patterns, and making small adjustments early – have started carrying over into my own life. I enjoy the quieter weekdays and fewer crowds when I’m out, and I’m fully present when spending time with my loved ones. 

-	Russell Chee, Operations Monitoring Centre #HumansofDBS

“Chinese New Year is usually when my kitchen is busiest. This year, on day three, it will be closed until nighttime.

That’s when Ramadhan, the first day of fasting, begins.

I’m a Malay Muslim, married into a Chinese family. We’ve been navigating two cultures, two faiths, and two festive celebrations together for more than 25 years now.

I still remember the first time my husband tried fasting – by midday, he began groaning, checking the time and acting like he hadn’t eaten for years.  When he finally managed to fast for a full day, he was genuinely proud of himself. 

I was proud of him too, because he made an effort to understand my faith even before we got married.

So when my first Chinese New Year celebration came around, I made sure to extend the same care. I read up on Chinese customs – no wearing black (even though it’s my favourite colour) because that colour was reserved for funerals, and no sweeping as it’s believed to sweep away good luck.

I spent the day in the kitchen with my mother-in-law, who kindly ensured all meats were halal-sourced. Cooking during festive seasons was already second nature to me, having done the same with my mum during Hari Raya since I was young. So I followed along, helping where I could. 

The two celebrations are actually quite similar. We gather as family. We eat together. We give out money packets (Ang Bao for Chinese New Year, and duit raya for Hari Raya).

During Covid-19, we started using DBS PayLah! and QR Ang Baos. At first, the elders expressed their preference for cash, but eventually, they appreciated the convenience. A win-win for us too, since we no longer have to physically exchange cash.  

I am so thankful for my family. There’s no feeling that compares to being understood – and that is something I strive to extend to the customers I work with at the bank. I joined DBS in 2002 as a customer service officer. Today, I’m with the Institutional Banking Group, assisting clients through the hotline. 

Over the years, I’ve moved across different banking segments, but the heart of the work has stayed the same. Being present, listening carefully, and doing the right thing for our customers. I strive to anticipate each of their unique needs, respecting their perspectives to build genuine trust and rapport.

This year, for our festive celebrations, there will be small accommodations, like having dinner earlier on the second day so everyone can rest to be able to prepare for sahur, the pre-dawn meal before fasting the next day.

It’s a simple adjustment to ensure we continue to show up for one another, just as we always have.”
 
- Bisharah Sairasi, Institutional Banking Group

“'I want to lay a trap.'

Alarm bells rang in my head when an elderly man walked into our branch at Suntec City Mall, muttering those words. He was frail, hunched and his eyes kept darting to his phone, which buzzed relentlessly. 

'They keep asking me for more money, I need to stop them,' he whispered hurriedly. To calm him down, my colleague Serene (pictured below) and I brought him into a private room to find out more. 

For days, he’d been receiving calls. The first claimed that he owed insurance payments. When he told them he had no such policy, he was transferred to someone else, who accused him of lying and money laundering.

'They told me I could be arrested!' he exclaimed frantically, drumming his fingers on the table anxiously. 'They said I had to prove I was clean!'

The caller then pressured him to withdraw his savings for an 'investigation'. The threats, the secrecy, the urgency - these were clear signs of a scam to us. 

But to him, the fear felt real. Just the day before, he withdrew SGD 15,000 and handed it to a stranger near his home. 

Then they demanded more.

'That’s when I thought something was not right, so I came to DBS for advice,' he said quietly, his eyes falling. 'If they are scammers targeting people like me, I want to catch them.'

We conducted our own internal checks with DBS’ anti-scam team, who confirmed that it was a scam, and encouraged him to call the police. They arrived at our branch soon after. 

Under their guidance, he answered one of the incoming scam calls, pretending to cooperate. We all huddled together in that room, as we overheard the scammer’s instructions - withdraw another SGD 15,000, send proof, and they’d reveal a location to meet.

To create the 'proof', Serene first tried preparing an envelope filled with vouchers to mimic a stack of cash, but the scammer insisted on a photo of real notes. 

With officers present, I accompanied him to the ATM, as he withdrew the money and took a picture of the cash. Right after that, I immediately helped him deposit every dollar back into his account.  

Satisfied with the 'proof', the scammer provided a meet-up point – and the police officers left quickly to apprehend the perpetrator. 

The next few minutes felt like hours, but we quickly received confirmation that the suspect was taken into custody. I watched as the elderly man breathed a huge sigh of relief. 

This case showed us the power of teamwork – from the victim wanting to stop the scammer, to DBS and the police working together. Seeing everyone step in at the right moment reminded me of why I come to work every day - to protect people when they feel most vulnerable, and to make sure they never face such situations alone.

This collaboration creates a powerful support system focusing on helping SMEs move from strategy to implementation. 
  
As market expectations shift, many are already adapting their digital and sustainability strategies to remain competitive. Through this collaboration, DBS and NYP will focus on practical support that SMEs can apply on the ground. This includes building capacity and capability in sustainability through the DBS ESG Ready Programme. This helps SMEs better understand, plan, finance and implement solutions suited to their needs. They will also be able to access skills and co-create innovation projects to address real business pain points. 
 
Transformation is a collective effort. By combining academic expertise with financial resources, we hope to provide SMEs with the right environment to test new ideas and scale their efforts effectively.

It takes 78-year-old Mr Lee approximately two hours to walk from his home to the closest market. 

With a walking stick in each hand and a fiery spirit of determination, he shuffles forward in small steps on most days to buy food (usually fish soup or pork rice) for his nephew, who is in his sixties. Due to mental health issues, he has been dependent on Mr Lee since he lost both parents over a decade ago.

Yet the three-room flat they share in the Whampoa estate had become increasingly cluttered and difficult to navigate. Belongings left behind by Mr Lee’s late sister and brother-in-law filled the home, carrying memories too painful to disturb.  

So for the pair, it was a leap of faith and courage to accept help when DBS staff offered them a housing makeover, as part of a home improvement programme organised by the Private Bank team in partnership with TOUCH Community Services, to support lower-income households with decluttering, cleaning and minor refurbishment works.  

Benjamin Quek, Team Lead for Client Experience and Employee Engagement at DBS Private Bank, has been spearheading the programme with his colleagues since its inception in 2019 under the bank’s People of Purpose employee volunteer movement. 

“When you visit these homes, you really understand how tough the conditions can be. I’ve seen bed bugs, mould and families doing their best in spaces that just aren’t safe or workable. And that’s why it’s very important for us to do what we can to help the community.”  

Over 10 months, Benjamin and volunteers from DBS spent time after working hours and on weekends with staff from TOUCH to coordinate five different home improvement projects, including sourcing new furniture - like beds and cupboards - to meet the residents' physical mobility needs. 

“The more difficult the housing conditions, the more you realise how much this support means,” Benjamin said. “And when you see the relief on a senior’s face after the makeover… that’s what keeps us going.”  

To cover even more ground, the team also invited a corporate client specialising in construction and property development, and 15 of their employees, to be part of the programme. 

Together with nearly 70 DBS staff, they scrubbed, repainted, decluttered, organised electrical installations and helped families like Mr Lee’s prepare to welcome 2026 more comfortably. 

Reflecting on the experience, Joseph Poon, Group Head of DBS Private Bank, shared, “How hard (it must be) to let someone (and strangers) into your personal space, to help you with (spring cleaning) items that you’ve held dear for so many years? But for Mr Lee to open his heart and give it a shot, and for us to be part of that, is something I’m really grateful for.”

Learn more about our bank-wide efforts to tackle the urgent challenges faced by the community. https://go.dbs.com/43jSBCS.

It’s the season of giving and second-life sparkle. With some creative reimagining, colleagues across our markets turned everyday discards into beautiful Christmas trees, continuing our annual DBS Giving Tree tradition that started in 2019. 

From coconut husk ornaments in Singapore and recycled bottles in China, to repurposed wooden pallets in Hong Kong, a cardboard tree with snack packs in Taiwan and trees made with everyday items like coffee stirrers and toilet rolls in India and Indonesia – these trees show that sustainability and festive cheer can go hand in hand.
 
From all of us at DBS, we wish you a Merry Christmas and Happy Holidays.
 
#DBSGivingTree #Christmas #Sustainability #Impact #circulardesign

What if a Christmas tree was designed to live many lives? 🌱🎄At DBS, that idea shapes how we design and celebrate.
 
At the centre of this year’s DBS Giving Tree are stackable stools handwoven using dried river grass. They form the base of the tree today, and will continue their use as office furniture after the festive season.
 
Since 2019, the DBS Giving Tree has reflected our circular design approach. Rather than creating something new for a season, we focus on extending the life of materials.
 
This year’s tree is built with a clear second life in mind. Co-created with our employees and designed with ipse ipsa ipsum, a Singapore-based sustainable furniture and materials studio, the tree brings together thoughtful design and shared effort.
 
Using discarded coconut husks, and repurposed materials, the tree will live on in new and practical ways beyond the festive season, just as last year’s mycelium baubles now light up spaces across DBS offices.
 
#DBSGivingTree #circulardesign

“There is a lot of focus on the negative side of Q-day (Quantum Day),” said DBS Chief Transformation and Data Officer, Nimish Panchmatia. “But I think it will also bring a lot of positives.”
 
Speaking on a panel at the recent Singapore Fintech Festival 2025, Nimish was referencing “Quantum Day” or “Q-day”, which is when quantum computers finally grow powerful enough to break public encryption.
 
While emphasising the need for the industry to come together to prepare for this technological event, Nimish shared that Q-day is not necessarily to be dreaded. We should instead think of how best we can take advantage of the tech, he said, sharing an example of how DBS is experimenting with using quantum for fraud transaction surveillance.
 
“Quantum is going to be a competitive differentiator. If you don’t do it, you’re going to get left behind,” said Nimish.
